source.title: Control Barrier Function Based Quadratic Programs for Safety Critical Systems

confidence_rationale: Provides the foundational formal framework for certifying that dynamical systems remain within a safe set -- directly applicable to Q-024's prescriptive variant ('certify bounded convergence'). CBF conditions are Lyapunov-like constraints on the system trajectory that guarantee forward invariance of the safe set; Zero Barrier Functions additionally make the set asymptotically stable (an attractor). This gives Q-024 a rigorous prescriptive representation. Confidence gap: REE's safe/ethical set may not be the kind of smooth superlevel set CBFs require, and the framework is for deterministic control-affine systems rather than stochastic learned policies.

mapping.source_claim_statement: A Control Barrier Function (CBF) h certifies forward invariance of the safe set C = {x: h(x) >= 0} if it satisfies a Lyapunov-like condition on its time derivative. A Zero Barrier Function (ZBF) additionally makes C asymptotically stable. Safety and stability can be unified in a Quadratic Program (QP) that arbitrates between CBF (safety) and CLF (stability) constraints in real time. If a CBF exists, a safety-critical controller is synthesizable.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-024 asks for a formal representation for threshold/feedback processes reliably reaching an emergent state q. The prescriptive variant ('certify bounded convergence') is exactly what a CBF provides: if h(x) >= 0 defines the ethical safe set (the set of trajectories consistent with bounded harm), then a CBF for h certifies that REE's dynamics keep the system in this set. The ZBF extension makes the ethical set an attractor -- meaning convergence to, not just persistence within, the bounded region. For Q-024's three-variant framing: prescriptive = CBF certificate; descriptive = characterizing the invariant set C geometrically; diagnostic = verifying whether a specific trajectory deviation moves h below zero.
mapping.mapping_caveat: CBFs require the safe set to be a smooth superlevel set of a continuously differentiable function h. REE's ethical/harm constraint region may not have this smooth geometry -- it could be defined by multiple intersecting constraints, discontinuities from catastrophic actions, or learned representations that are not differentiable. The framework is for deterministic control-affine dynamical systems; REE's stochastic, policy-based dynamics require stochastic CBF extensions (not covered by this paper). MECH-127's counterfactual utility makes the harm constraint depend on hypothetical trajectories, not just the current state -- this is harder to encode in a simple superlevel set.
mapping.source_context: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 2017; formal control theory; nonlinear control-affine systems with real-time QP-based controller synthesis

confidence_components.notes: IEEE TAC flagship paper with extremely high source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate because CBFs require smooth safe sets and deterministic dynamics; REE's constraint geometry and stochastic learning dynamics may require stochastic or non-smooth CBF extensions. Transfer risk from deterministic robotics/control to learned AI policy dynamics is real but not disqualifying -- the formal structure is directly translatable.
